summary.intent: Implement a terminal disable feature for the moltis web UI (Issue #681) and then comprehensively harden the authentication/remote access security of the application against brute-force and other attacks.
summary.outcome: Terminal disable feature was implemented and merged via PR #682. A LoginGuard with IP banning and account lockout was added. All 9 security issues (setup code brute-force, API key scope enforcement, HSTS, CSP, session limits, API key salted hashing, password minimum, setup code expiry, auth audit log) were implemented, tested, and committed.

repo: Config lives under MoltisConfig with a ServerConfig section — the right place for server-level feature flags like terminal_enabled, not under [tools.web].
Environment variables (e.g. MOLTIS_TERMINAL_DISABLED) are used as deployment-level hard overrides that win over TOML config, following the pattern of MOLTIS_VERSION.
Gon pattern is used to inject server-side config into the frontend — fields like voice_enabled, graphql_enabled serve as the model for terminal_enabled.
API key scopes are stored in the database but were NOT enforced at the middleware level — only key existence was checked in auth_middleware.rs.
The auth_gate middleware inserts AuthIdentity into request extensions; it can be read via axum::Extension<AuthIdentity> in handlers.
RequireAdmin extractor only works with AuthState (which has FromRef impls), not AppState — scope enforcement on config routes required a different approach via the handler directly.
unsafe code is entirely denied by lint rules in this repo — std::env::set_var cannot be used in tests.
CUDA-dependent crates cause pre-existing build failures on macOS; clippy/tests must be run with --exclude flags for those crates.
The setup code was a 6-digit numeric code with no expiry and no rate limit — a significant brute-force risk on public deployments.
Sessions had no per-user limit, enabling resource exhaustion and slow revocation.
API keys used plain SHA-256 (no salt), inconsistent with Argon2 used for passwords.

code.finding: ServerConfig is the correct struct for terminal_enabled; is_terminal_enabled() checks MOLTIS_TERMINAL_DISABLED env var first then falls back to config value.
Three terminal routes need guarding: GET /api/terminal/windows, POST /api/terminal/windows, and GET /api/terminal/ws (WebSocket upgrade). The windows handlers lacked State(state) and needed it added.
resolve_client_ip was private; needed to be made pub to share with login_guard.rs.

New module implementing IP banning (10 failures → escalating bans: 5m, 10m, 20m... up to 24h cap) and account lockout (3 distinct IPs fail same account within 15 min → 15 min lockout). IpRecord needs a last_failure_at field for stale entry cleanup, otherwise entries with consecutive_failures > 0 but no ban leak memory forever.
AuthIdentity needed a scopes field added; all construction sites needed updating. Session auth gets empty scopes vec, API key auth populates from DB.

require_config_access needed an optional identity + require_admin bool to enforce operator.admin scope on write endpoints (config_save, restart). Used axum::Extension<AuthIdentity> extractor to get identity in handlers.

API key hashing upgraded to HMAC-SHA256 with random salt; backward-compatible with legacy unsalted keys via fallback lookup. Max session limit enforced during session creation. Audit log methods added for auth events.
GatewayInner needed setup_code_created_at: Instant field to support 30-minute setup code expiry.

workflow: Beads issue tracking system is used — claim issue before starting, close when done, used for both individual features and epics.
Background compilation checks (Bash run_in_background) are used during implementation to catch errors asynchronously.
Test passwords used in integration tests must be updated when password minimum length changes — HTTP handler tests use literal JSON bodies.
taplo fmt is used for TOML formatting in addition to rustfmt.
Clippy collapsible-if warnings are common when adding nested guards — collapse them before final commit.
worktrees are used for feature branches: /Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/<branch-name>/

friction: CUDA crates cause pre-existing build failures on macOS, requiring --exclude flags on every cargo clippy/test invocation.
unsafe code ban prevents direct env var manipulation in tests — the env var override path for is_terminal_enabled could not be unit tested.
RequireAdmin extractor couldn't be used on AppState routes (no FromRef impls), forcing a more verbose per-handler approach with axum::Extension<AuthIdentity>.
Multiple rounds of clippy collapsible-if fixes needed across login_guard.rs, auth_routes.rs, and tools_routes.rs.

The cleanup test for LoginGuard initially failed because entries with consecutive_failures > 0 but no ban were retained indefinitely — required adding last_failure_at field to fix the memory leak and the test.
Five integration tests failed after raising the password minimum from 8 to 12 characters — test fixture passwords needed updating.
The audit log methods were initially placed outside the impl block due to brace nesting, requiring a fix.
open_items: Scope enforcement only covers config_save and restart — other admin-sensitive endpoints (API key management, user management) may still lack scope checks.
Legacy API keys using plain SHA-256 (no salt) remain valid via fallback — no migration path to force re-issuance of old keys.
No audit log retention/rotation policy — the auth_audit_log table will grow unbounded.
The setup code brute-force fix uses the same LoginGuard as login, but setup code itself is still 6 numeric digits (900K combinations) — upgrading to alphanumeric would improve entropy.
CSP and HSTS headers were added but not audited for correctness/completeness (e.g. CSP directives, HSTS max-age value, includeSubDomains).
No frontend feedback when a user is locked out vs. wrong password — both return 429 but the UX could distinguish them.
Account lockout from distributed IPs (3 distinct IPs) has no admin override/unlock mechanism — a locked account can only self-recover after 15 minutes.
The audit log is not exposed via any API endpoint — no way for admins to review auth events from the UI.
